title: Umi Meets Adachi
description: A chance encounter at a Chinese restaurant. Umi, the narrator, meets a girl in a China dress—reluctant, awkward, and strikingly beautiful. A brief, quiet moment of connection.
source: My First Love's Kiss Volume 1: Melonbooks Bonus
content:
To put it simply, a lot happened. But 'a lot' doesn't mean anything worth listing one by one. What mattered was that because of all those things—things that felt almost like shackles—I had to handle dinner alone tonight.

Money wasn't the problem. It was just… I'd barely ever eaten out, so restaurants made me feel weirdly intimidated. I'd hover near the bright storefronts, peek inside, then slowly back away. Even though my hands were empty, there was no one around now to drag me forward.

'... I'm so stupid.'

I wasn't a kid anymore. Why couldn't I handle something this simple?

Thinking that, I wandered through a town sinking deeper into night. My feet stopped without me realizing it. A sign, bright red and yellow, caught my attention. I cut diagonally across the empty parking lot and checked the place from outside. Didn't see any crowd inside.

Looks like this Chinese restaurant is pretty quiet. Fine—this'll do.

'Oh, finally! A customer!'

The moment I stepped in, someone barked at me in a tone that didn't sound welcoming at all. I froze. A middle-aged woman and a much younger-looking girl approached.

'Welcome~!'

She called out like someone working a street stall… Yeah, probably like a street stall. Funny—I'd never actually been to one. Where did I even get that mental image?

The older woman looked strong, and her accent was unusual—maybe she wasn't Japanese. The other girl was wearing a Chinese-style outfit—a China dress, I think? She had black hair, probably my age or maybe a year younger… Though honestly, with how tiny I am, I don't look older than anyone.

She kept tugging anxiously at the hem of her dress, as if worried about how much skin it showed.

'Use her to practice taking customers.'

'Ehh…?'

The one who seemed like the manager tossed out that confusing instruction. The girl in the China dress clearly disliked the idea. But the manager pushed her lightly toward me and went straight back to the kitchen.

'Put some energy into it.' With that last order, the girl reluctantly approached me.

Her dress was a striking bright blue, but her expression was completely gloomy.

'Please… have a seat.'

Her lips moved only as much as absolutely necessary, and her eyes held no emotion whatsoever.

'Oh… sure.'

I had no right to judge, but wow—her attitude was pretty poor. The place was empty, so I took the seat closest to the entrance. She put down the menu and retreated to the wall like she was trying to disappear. At first I wondered if the manager was scolding her and whether I should worry—but then I realized there was nothing I could do anyway, so I opened the menu.

Shark fin at that price? Does anyone actually order that? When I looked up, she came over immediately. After I ordered fried rice, she repeated it back in a tiny voice, then fled to the kitchen. I heard them talking inside, and soon I saw her standing against the wall again.

'I saw that~!'

The manager's booming voice echoed from the kitchen. Sure, I was the only customer—but was this really fine?

The girl let out a long sigh, then, looking like she'd given up on life, came back toward me. So she couldn't escape after all. Why did she have to practice on me? I knew it was because there were no other customers, but still… maybe I really had chosen the wrong place.

She leaned over slightly, looking down at me. With a twitchy face and stiff voice, she said:

'Wel… come… to our restaurant~'

A bit late, considering I already sat down.

'Smile! Smile!'

The manager shouted like an onlooker at a festival. The girl pressed her lips tight and kept tugging at her dress. I think I overheard a muttered complaint about 'making me wear something like this…'

Okay, I was starting to understand the situation. But it wasn't my problem, right? If she didn't improve, was this going to go on forever? Also—not important—but judging by how she held the tray, she was probably left-handed.

'Um…'

'Would you like to order something else~?'

She asked something completely unreasonable in a stiff, mechanical voice. Did this place expect customers to order one new dish every time they opened their mouth?

She was just standing there, hovering. Impossible to ignore, and honestly… annoying.

'I mean… look.' I started speaking.

'You don't have to smile. Just, like… as long as you can talk clearly, that should be enough.'

Her face didn't even suit smiling that much anyway—though that was just my personal impression.

'When you talk, it feels like you're not really directing your words at anyone. If you fix that… it should be fine.'

I could see it in her eyes: no interest in other people. Not caring is fine—everyone has their own way of living. But in this kind of job, you probably have to hide the real you a little.

'…And straighten your back.'

'Well said~!'

The manager yelled from afar. The girl looked back toward the voice, froze for a moment, then looked at me again. Just as I'd said, she straightened her posture slightly. Her eyes changed shape—she focused her gaze, really looked at me.

And when our eyes met like that, I finally noticed how strikingly pretty she was.

She was so pretty that a selfish thought crossed my mind—I absolutely didn't want that person to see her. I sighed at my own foolishness.

Lately, dumb thoughts like that just came to me instantly. The girl, unaware of any of that, asked me in a clear, natural voice:

'May I take your order?'

Didn't I already order?

I nodded anyway.

'What a strange restaurant…'

Probably… I won't come back. Still, seeing someone wearing a China dress for the first time—that was interesting, I guess.

Overstuffed from dinner, my brain felt sluggish as I walked. Ahead of me, across the glow of the streetlights, I saw someone waving. There was only one person it could be.

'Oh, Mom.'

We hadn't contacted each other, but of course she'd just show up out of nowhere. Things like this happen often. Maybe mothers and daughters just naturally walk in the same direction.

'I found a place you can stay, so I came to get you.'

'Oh?'

That was fast. Mom faced forward, smiling brightly. A smile that made her look… extremely unreliable.

'This time it's definitely a good place!'

'Mmm…'

It was the first time she'd ever hyped up the place I'd be lodging at. That alone piqued my interest a little. I didn't know how to make myself feel optimistic, but—

'…Sure. I hope so.'

If I could just have a little more time not worrying about other people's eyes—a little more freedom—that was enough.

And so, like always, Mom and I set off in search of a place to live. Whether her 'definitely' would prove true—that was something only time could answer.
